trajectory_summary: The call was lost at the intro. Tyler's opening framing ('I have received a request') directly triggered Didi's first objection ('I didn't send you a request'), and every subsequent exchange was damage management rather than recovery. The call de-escalated from hostile to cordial, but that de-escalation was Didi's generosity, not Tyler's skill. No qualification, no bill anchor, no appointment — the call ended as a list-removal interaction.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as unknown. Based on the transcript, the correct tag is DQ/no-appointment — Didi explicitly refused interest, Tyler offered list removal, and the call ended without any booking attempt or qualification data captured. There is no ambiguity in the outcome.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Intro framing — do not assert customer actions the customer may not recognize
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I have received a request to look at that solar for the home.
primary_coaching_focus.why: This single line created the adversarial open. Tyler's improvised 'I have received a request' framing made a claim about Didi's prior behavior that she immediately contradicted, and the call never recovered. Neither schema intro variant makes this error. The manager should drill both intro variants with Tyler until the framing is automatic — this is a repeatable structural failure that will recur on every call where Tyler improvises the opening.

key_moments.quote: I have received a request to look at that solar for the home.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the root cause of the entire call's failure. Neither Ion intro variant ('certified solar installer for Tesla in {state}' or 'reaching out about the solar form you filled out online') makes a claim about a 'request' the customer sent. Tyler's improvised framing asserted a customer action Didi did not recognize, which made her first words a direct contradiction. The causal chain is direct: intro language ('I have received a request') → Didi's objection ('I didn't send you a request') → no recovery path available. Every subsequent exchange was downstream of this single framing error. Strategic read is not available here — there is no later move this intro was setting up.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. No. My apologies. I can take you off our list. You didn't fill out any information online looking into it? Or
key_moments.stewart_read: Didi's 'I don't need it and I don't want it' at 00:15 is a hello-objection — the schema protocol calls for acknowledging and asking for 30 seconds, not immediate capitulation. Tyler's response skips the small-ask entirely and goes straight to list removal. The single probe ('You didn't fill out any information online?') is structurally correct but arrives after the concession, making it feel like a last-ditch check rather than a confident reframe. Reactive read holds: Tyler did not attempt the protocol-defined response before conceding.

key_moments.quote: Mhmm. And they picked up my address off of that. That's the only thing I can figure out.
key_moments.stewart_read: Didi is constructing Ion's legitimacy narrative herself — she is theorizing that an ad interaction led to her information being shared, which is exactly the honest explanation the schema protocol calls for. Tyler's response ('No. You're okay. Well, yeah, I'll take you off our list') wastes the opening entirely. This was the clearest available moment to validate Didi's theory, confirm how Ion received her information, and use that specificity to reduce suspicion. The schema is explicit: specificity reduces suspicion, vagueness increases it. Tyler chose vagueness.

key_moments.quote: I don't know if you got somebody on your end of it that's causing problems or what.
key_moments.stewart_read: Didi is now speculating that Ion itself may be the source of the problem — an internal bad actor or system error. This is a trust-damaging hypothesis that Tyler could have addressed directly and specifically. Instead, Tyler deflects ('No problem') and repeats the list-removal offer. The friction has not been resolved; it has been acknowledged and bypassed. The schema directive on friction resolution asks whether the rep solved, defended, or dismissed — Tyler dismissed.
